error_handling.overview: Error scenarios and recovery strategies for Debug mode.

scenario.name: unable_to_reproduce_autonomously
scenario.description: In hands-off mode, cannot reproduce the failure autonomously (e.g., need a specific environment or state that can't be recreated)
scenario.trigger: All autonomous reproduction attempts fail — running tests doesn't trigger the bug, and command execution can't replicate the conditions.

recovery.step: Document what was attempted: which commands were run, what was observed, why reproduction failed
Document what is needed: the specific conditions or environment required to reproduce

Report via completion summary: "Cannot reproduce autonomously. Reproduction requires: [conditions]. What was attempted: [list]. The investigation is incomplete — spawner may need to provide a different environment or invoke Debug in interactive mode."
Do NOT ask the user for help — hands-off mode forbids it. Escalate to spawner.

scenario.name: test_suite_has_pre_existing_failures
scenario.description: Running existing tests reveals failures that were present before Debug was spawned
scenario.trigger: Unit or integration tests fail, and the failures are unrelated to Debug's fix

recovery.step: Investigate each pre-existing failure using the same diagnostic-first approach
Fix each failure that is within scope. Debug leaves things greener.

If a pre-existing failure is too large in scope: - Too large means: requires changes to 5+ files, database schema changes, API contract changes, or architectural restructuring. - Document: the failing test, its root cause (if identified), why it's out of scope, and what kind of change is needed. - Mark as "documented out-of-scope failure." - Continue with the remaining workflow. - Documented out-of-scope failures are excluded from the all-green requirement at step 8. - If in doubt whether something is too large: fix it.
Continue with the remaining workflow after addressing or documenting all failures

guardrail.rule: Do not ignore pre-existing failures silently — either fix them or document them
Do not get stuck indefinitely on a pre-existing failure that requires architectural changes

scenario.name: circuit_breaker_same_test_failing
scenario.description: The same test keeps failing after 3 genuinely different fix approaches
scenario.trigger: After 3 cycles of: diagnose → apply a genuinely different fix → run tests → same test still fails. Each test tracks its own counter. Step 5 and step 8 share the same counter for the same test. Genuinely different means targeting different root cause hypotheses (e.g., data layer vs. business logic vs. presentation). NOT genuinely different: incremental timeout changes, constant value tweaks, increasingly broad try/catch.

recovery.step: Stop immediately. Do NOT attempt a 4th fix.
Document: the suspected root cause, all 3 approaches tried, why each failed, the current test output
In hands-off mode: report via completion summary. Spawner handles escalation.

In interactive mode: present findings to user. "The test [name] still fails after 3 different approaches. Here's what was tried: [summary]. How would you like to proceed?" Options: "Investigate further with fresh perspective", "Skip this test for now", "I'll investigate manually"

prevention.item: Ensure each fix attempt is genuinely different — not variations of the same approach
Use mandatory instrumentation on retry (no guessing twice without evidence)
Consider whether the test itself might be wrong — but do NOT modify E2E tests

scenario.name: multi_issue_partial_circuit_breaker
scenario.description: During multi-issue fixing, one issue hits the circuit breaker while others are already verified
scenario.trigger: In the multi-issue per-issue loop, issue N hits the 3-attempt circuit breaker. Issues 1 through N-1 are already verified.

recovery.step: Preserve all previously-verified fixes — do NOT revert them
Write regression tests for all verified fixes immediately
Run the full test suite for the verified fixes to confirm they're green
Report partial success: "[N-1] issues fixed with regression tests. [1] issue unresolved: [description]. Circuit breaker triggered after 3 approaches: [summary]."
Do NOT lose verified work. Do NOT continue trying the unresolved issue.
Do NOT proceed to batch regression tests for the unresolved issue.

scenario.name: fix_introduces_regression
scenario.description: After applying a fix, running existing tests reveals NEW failures (tests that passed before now fail)
scenario.trigger: Tests that previously passed now fail after applying the fix

recovery.step: Analyze the new failures — which tests broke and why?

Determine the correct response based on test type: - E2E test broken: the fix is wrong. The E2E test is the authority on user-visible behavior. Refine the fix. Do NOT modify the E2E test. - Unit/integration test that genuinely tests correct behavior: the fix introduced a bug. Refine the fix. - Unit/integration test that depended on the buggy behavior: the test expectations were wrong, not the fix. Update the test expectations to match the correct behavior. This is ONLY acceptable for non-E2E tests.
Apply the refined fix or updated test
Re-run all tests
If the same regression persists after 3 attempts: escalate (circuit breaker)

scenario.name: instrumentation_output_unavailable
scenario.description: In interactive mode, user cannot provide the requested diagnostic output (wrong location, tool unavailable, etc.)
scenario.trigger: User responds that they can't access the debug output

recovery.step: Verify the output location guidance: "The [DEBUG] output should appear in [location]. Are you able to see it there?"
If user confirms the location is correct but no output appears: the instrumented code path isn't being executed. Add instrumentation to earlier entry points and ask user to reproduce again.
If the output location is wrong for the user's environment: provide alternative locations or output methods (e.g., write to a log file instead of console)

If the user's environment genuinely cannot provide diagnostic output (no console, no log capability, environment restrictions): try alternative investigation methods — static analysis, adding more assertions to tests, process of elimination.

If this scenario occurs during a mandatory-instrumentation retry (step 3): follow step 3's escape clause — state clearly that instrumentation was mandated but impossible, and investigation proceeded via alternative methods.

scenario.name: multi_issue_later_fix_breaks_earlier
scenario.description: In multi-issue mode, fixing issue N+1 causes a previously-verified issue N to regress
scenario.trigger: After fixing issue N+1 and running the combined test suite (step 2b), the previously-passing tests for issue N fail

recovery.step: Investigate: did the fix for N+1 genuinely break N, or was N's fix incomplete?
Re-fix issue N with the new code state in mind
Re-run the combined test suite (step 2b)
Continue with the remaining issues
If fixes keep interfering with each other: the issues may be more deeply related than initially assessed. Re-triage: should they be fixed together as a single, more complex fix?

scenario.name: e2e_fix_requires_modifying_e2e_test
scenario.description: An E2E test is failing because the test itself has a bug (wrong selector, outdated expectation, timing issue)
scenario.trigger: The E2E test failure is clearly in the test code, not the application code

recovery.step: Document the specific issue in the E2E test: what's wrong, why it's wrong, what the correct expectation should be
Do NOT modify the E2E test directly — E2E tests are the authority
Do NOT fix the application code to match a buggy E2E test — that would introduce bugs into the application

Report via completion summary: "The E2E test [name] appears to have a bug in the test itself: [description]. This requires the test author to fix — Debug does not modify E2E tests. The application code behavior is correct."
